chunks.text: Murat Arcak is a Professor at UC Berkeley's EECS department. His research focuses on control theory, autonomous systems, and multi-agent systems with applications in transportation, energy, and biology. He has received numerous awards including the NSF CAREER Award (2003), Donald P. Eckman Award (2006), SIAM Control and Systems Theory Prize (2007), and IEEE Antonio Ruberti Young Researcher Prize (2014). He is a member of SIAM and fellow of IEEE and IFAC.

Murat Arcak earned his Ph.D. in Electrical Engineering from UC Santa Barbara in 2000, his M.S. from the same institution in 1997, and his B.S. from Bogazici University in Istanbul, Turkey in 1996. His research areas at Berkeley include Control, Intelligent Systems, and Robotics (CIR); Cyber-Physical Systems and Design Automation (CPSDA); Biosystems & Computational Biology (BIO); and Power and Energy (ENE).

Murat Arcak is affiliated with several research centers at UC Berkeley including the Berkeley Artificial Intelligence Research Lab (BAIR), Berkeley Deep Drive (BDD), Center for Theoretical Foundations (CLIMB), Institute of Transportation Studies (ITS), and Partners for Advanced Transit and Highways (PATH). He has published extensively in top journals including IEEE Transactions on Automatic Control, Nature Communications, and Automatica.

Selected publications include work on data-driven reachability estimation (IEEE TAC 2023), synthetic genetic feedback optimizers (Nature Communications 2023), interval reachability analysis (Springer 2021), optogenetic cell signaling (Nature Communications 2020), symmetry reduction for dynamic programming (Automatica 2018), traffic flow control (IEEE Control Systems 2017), and networks of dissipative systems (Springer 2016).

Murat Arcak has received numerous honors including the 2021 Brockett-Willems Outstanding Paper Award, 2020 IFAC Fellow and Automatica Paper Prize, 2017 CSS Transactions Outstanding Paper Award, 2014 Electrical Engineering Teaching Award, 2012 IEEE Fellow, 2007 SIAM Control and Systems Theory Prize, 2006 Donald P. Eckman Award, and 2003 NSF CAREER Award. His contact information includes office location at 569 Cory Hall and email arcak@berkeley.edu.
